prompt: NFPA 11 covers:

options.text: Residential dwelling unit sprinkler retrofit only
Low-, medium-, and high-expansion foam systems for Class B and certain special hazards
Carbon dioxide total flooding concentration tables exclusively
Kitchen hood grease duct welding procedures only

explanation: NFPA 11 addresses foam water solution proportioning, discharge devices, and system design for flammable liquid hazards.

distractorExplanations.d: Kitchen systems are NFPA 96 with listed UL 300 appliances.

prompt: A balanced pressure foam proportioning system maintains:

options.text: Higher water pressure than concentrate by exactly 50 psi at all flows
Zero foam concentrate use during system flushing
Equal pressure on foam concentrate and water inlets to the proportioner for accurate metering
Separate fire pump curves for each foam chamber without reference to water supply

explanation: Balanced pressure systems stabilize proportioning across flow ranges — common in aircraft hangar and tank farm protection.

distractorExplanations.a: Equal, not fixed 50 psi differential, is the design goal.
distractorExplanations.b: Pump sizing still relates to combined foam solution demand.
distractorExplanations.d: Pump sizing still relates to combined foam solution demand.

prompt: High-expansion foam is particularly suited to:

options.text: Precision discharge on energized computer circuit boards
Ordinary combustible paper storage without flammable liquids
Subsurface injection into underground gasoline tanks exclusively
Filling large volumes such as warehouses or LNG impoundments to separate fuel from air

explanation: High-expansion foam generates voluminous bubbles for total flooding of enclosures and vapor suppression.

distractorExplanations.a: Energized electronics need non-conductive agents, not high-expansion foam flooding.
distractorExplanations.b: Class A paper may use sprinklers; high-expansion targets vapor/air separation.
distractorExplanations.c: Class A paper may use sprinklers; high-expansion targets vapor/air separation.

prompt: NFPA 12 addresses:

options.text: Carbon dioxide fire extinguishing systems including total flooding and local application
Wet chemical kitchen suppression only
Standpipe hose thread standards exclusively
Smoke control fan wiring in atriums only

explanation: NFPA 12 covers CO2 system design, concentrations, safety lockouts, and ventilation warnings for occupied areas.

prompt: Before discharging CO2 into an normally occupied enclosure, NFPA 12 requires:

options.text: No warning because CO2 is odorless and harmless to humans
Predischarge alarms and time delay or personnel accounting to allow evacuation
Immediate discharge upon any heat detector activation without delay
Manual discharge only from outside without any automatic detection

explanation: CO2 is asphyxiant; lockout and warning devices protect occupants from lethal oxygen displacement.

distractorExplanations.a: CO2 is hazardous to life at extinguishing concentrations.
distractorExplanations.c: Automatic discharge is permitted with life safety safeguards.
distractorExplanations.d: Automatic systems are common with required safety interlocks.

prompt: A total flooding CO2 system design concentration depends primarily on:

options.text: Ceiling sprinkler density from NFPA 13 only
Number of standpipe outlets per floor
Fuel type, enclosure volume, and minimum design concentration from NFPA 12 tables
Domestic water fixture unit count

explanation: CO2 quantity = concentration × adjusted volume; deep-seated fires may need extended holding periods.

distractorExplanations.a: Sprinkler density does not size CO2 concentration.
distractorExplanations.b: Sprinkler density does not size CO2 concentration.

prompt: Clean agent systems covered under NFPA 2001 are characterized by:

options.text: Water mist only with no gaseous phase
Dry chemical powder permanently coating all surfaces
Fuel shutoff valves exclusively without extinguishing medium
Electrically nonconductive gaseous agents that extinguish by heat absorption and/or oxygen reduction without residue

explanation: Clean agents (HFCs, FK-5-1-12, inert gases) protect sensitive equipment in enclosures with concentration-based design.

distractorExplanations.b: Dry chemical leaves residue; clean agents minimize residue.
distractorExplanations.c: Dry chemical leaves residue; clean agents minimize residue.

prompt: Room integrity (door fan) testing for clean agent enclosures verifies:

options.text: Sufficient agent retention time at design concentration by limiting excessive leakage
Sprinkler coverage in adjacent corridors
Structural fire resistance rating of exterior walls only
Adequate lighting levels for maintenance staff

explanation: Hold time ensures fire does not reignite; excessive leakage requires sealing or higher agent quantity.

distractorExplanations.b: Fire resistance is building code; integrity is leakage for agent hold.
distractorExplanations.c: Lighting is unrelated to agent retention.
distractorExplanations.d: Sprinkler coverage in corridors is separate protection.

prompt: Inergen (inert gas blend) extinguishing systems reduce oxygen concentration to approximately:

options.text: 5% oxygen matching deep-sea diving mixtures
12–15% by volume, below combustion support for many fuels while permitting human exposure briefly during egress
21% oxygen with no change from ambient air
0% oxygen achieved by vacuuming the room

explanation: Inert agents lower O₂ to extinguishing level while staying above immediate lethality for brief exposure — still requiring evacuation.

distractorExplanations.d: Vacuum is not a clean agent deployment method.

prompt: NFPA 17 covers:

options.text: Foam proportioning for municipal hydrants only
Residential 13D sprinkler pipe sizing only
Dry chemical extinguishing systems for local application and total flooding of specific hazards
Emergency voice evacuation intelligibility testing

explanation: NFPA 17 addresses dry chemical hardware, hazards, and recharge for flammable liquid and gas applications.

prompt: NFPA 17A applies to:

options.text: High-pressure CO2 localized rotor cooling
Underground diesel fuel tank cathodic protection
Smoke detector spacing in sleeping rooms
Wet chemical extinguishing systems, including many commercial kitchen hood applications

explanation: NFPA 17A covers wet chemical systems that saponify cooking oils — paired with UL 300 listed appliances.

prompt: A UL 300-listed kitchen fire suppression system requires:

options.text: Nozzles protecting hood, plenum, and duct; fusible links or detection; manual pull; fuel/power shutoff interlocks
Only a single water sprinkler above the fryer with no chemical agent
Discharge of halon 1301 as the only permitted agent
Annual testing by disabling fuel shutoff interlocks permanently

explanation: Modern cooking oils need wet chemical saponification; UL 300 reflects current appliance and fuel loads.

distractorExplanations.b: Halon is not used in new kitchen systems.
distractorExplanations.c: Halon is not used in new kitchen systems.
distractorExplanations.d: Fuel/power shutoff interlocks must remain functional.

prompt: The primary extinguishing mechanism of wet chemical kitchen systems on grease fires is:

options.text: Dilution of grease to non-flammable concentrations with water only
Saponification forming a foam blanket that separates grease from oxygen and cools surfaces
Displacement of all oxygen in the entire building by CO2
Increasing combustion temperature to burn grease completely

explanation: Wet chemicals convert burning fat to soap-like foam; water-only on hot grease can spread fire.

distractorExplanations.a: Water alone can cause grease fire spread and is insufficient.
distractorExplanations.c: Building-wide CO2 flooding is not kitchen system design.

prompt: Antifreeze loops in sprinkler systems per NFPA 13 must use:

options.text: Automotive ethylene glycol without listing regardless of concentration
Salt water brine as a permanent substitute in all climates
Listed antifreeze solutions meeting concentration and corrosion limits for the minimum use temperature
Any glycol mixture above 70% without engineering review

explanation: Listed antifreeze protects small unheated portions; recent editions restrict unlisted glycols due to flammability and corrosion.

distractorExplanations.a: Automotive antifreeze is not automatically acceptable.
distractorExplanations.b: Automotive antifreeze is not automatically acceptable.
distractorExplanations.d: High concentrations require compliance with listing and hazard review.

prompt: A pressure relief valve on a wet sprinkler system riser protects against:

options.text: Loss of all system pressure during normal fire flow
Corrosion inside dry pipe valves only
Excessive airflow in preaction supervisory piping
Trap pressure from thermal expansion of water when the system is isolated and heated

explanation: Heated water expands; without relief, trapped pressure can damage fittings — common above check valves.

distractorExplanations.a: Fire flow reduces pressure; relief addresses over-pressurization.
distractorExplanations.b: Dry valve corrosion is maintenance, not thermal expansion.
distractorExplanations.c: Dry valve corrosion is maintenance, not thermal expansion.

prompt: The fire department connection (FDC) on a sprinkler/standpipe system:

options.text: Allows the fire service to supplement water into the system at pumped pressure
Replaces the water supply tank volume calculation entirely
Serves as the primary drain point for all annual trip tests
Must be located inside the fire pump room exclusively

explanation: FDC inlets provide supplemental attack water; signage and threading must match local FD practice.

distractorExplanations.b: Drains are separate; FDC is an inlet.
distractorExplanations.c: Drains are separate; FDC is an inlet.
distractorExplanations.d: FDC is typically exterior accessible to apparatus.

prompt: A forward-flow test of a backflow preventer on a fire protection supply verifies:

options.text: Paint adhesion on exterior standpipe cabinets
That the assembly allows required fire flow without excessive pressure loss while preventing backflow
Voice evacuation audibility on the opposite wing
Sprinkler bulb color coding in storage

explanation: Annual forward-flow ensures the backflow device does not choke fire demand — critical for combined domestic/fire services.

distractorExplanations.a: Bulb color is manufacturer temperature rating, not backflow test.
distractorExplanations.c: Bulb color is manufacturer temperature rating, not backflow test.

prompt: Extra Hazard Group 2 occupancies per NFPA 13 include examples such as:

options.text: Light office paper storage in open cubicles only
Sleeping rooms in one- and two-family dwellings
Flammable liquid spraying, plastics processing, and other high heat release operations
Parking garages with only passenger vehicles and no fuel dispensing

explanation: EH2 has high heat release and rapid fire growth; design density 0.40 gpm/ft² over 2,500 ft² is typical baseline.

distractorExplanations.a: Office cubicles are usually light or ordinary hazard.
distractorExplanations.b: Ordinary parking may be OH1/OH2 depending on fuel load.
distractorExplanations.d: Ordinary parking may be OH1/OH2 depending on fuel load.

prompt: Sidewall sprinklers are typically used where:

options.text: Only underground vault protection is required
Deluge open nozzles are prohibited by code
Standpipe Class II hose stations replace all ceiling sprinklers
Pendent sprinklers cannot be installed due to obstructions or architectural limits along walls

explanation: Sidewall sprinklers have specific coverage limits and must be listed for sidewall application.

distractorExplanations.a: Underground vaults use different suppression strategies.
distractorExplanations.b: Deluge is separate; sidewalls are closed heat-responsive heads.
distractorExplanations.c: Deluge is separate; sidewalls are closed heat-responsive heads.

prompt: The main drain test on a sprinkler system riser is performed to:

options.text: Verify water supply adequacy and identify obstructions by measuring flow at the drain connection
Calibrate heat detector spacing in adjacent rooms
Prove foam concentrate viscosity annually
Set clean agent design concentration automatically

explanation: Main drain flow compared to acceptance baseline indicates supply deterioration or valve obstruction.

distractorExplanations.d: Clean agent concentration uses agent quantity and volume.

prompt: NFPA 13R systems in residential occupancies up to four stories differ from NFPA 13 in that:

options.text: 13R requires ESFR in every closet and bathroom without exception
13R permits omissions of sprinklers in certain areas and has distinct design objectives focused on life safety
13R mandates double-interlock preaction throughout
13R eliminates all fire department connection requirements nationally

explanation: NFPA 13R balances cost and life safety in residential mid-rise; not equivalent to full 13 commodity protection.

distractorExplanations.a: ESFR is storage protection, not 13R residential.
distractorExplanations.c: Preaction is special hazard protection, not 13R default.
distractorExplanations.d: FDC requirements depend on codes and AHJ; 13R does not universally eliminate FDC.

prompt: A looped automatic sprinkler feed main is hydraulically advantageous because:

options.text: It eliminates the need for any elevation head calculation
It guarantees all sprinklers operate simultaneously
It can supply water from two directions, reducing friction loss to remote branches
It replaces listed sprinklers with open pipes

explanation: Looping provides redundancy of path; calculations still determine realistic simultaneous flows.

distractorExplanations.d: Open pipes would be deluge, not looped wet mains.

prompt: Water mist systems per NFPA 750 differ from standard sprinklers primarily by:

options.text: Using only dry chemical powder in a water carrier
Requiring no water supply because mist is generated chemically
Operating exclusively at 1,000 psi without listing
Discharging fine water droplets with high surface area for cooling and oxygen displacement at lower total water flow

explanation: Water mist is a distinct technology with listed hardware and applications in machinery and heritage spaces.

distractorExplanations.b: Water mist still requires water supply.
distractorExplanations.c: Water mist still requires water supply.

prompt: Hazard classification under NFPA 13 for storage occupancies considers:

options.text: Commodity class, storage height, pallet type, and aisle width — not office occupancy labels alone
Only the building's historical landmark designation
Interior designer color palette of rack uprights
Number of parking spaces in the site plan

explanation: Storage chapters classify commodities (Class I–IV, plastics, idle pallets) to select design criteria.

distractorExplanations.d: Parking counts do not set storage commodity class.

prompt: When designing sprinklers for high-ceiling storage with solid shelving, the designer must:

options.text: Use light hazard spacing because the ceiling is far from commodities
Apply obstructed storage rules, potentially increasing sprinkler density or adding in-rack protection
Remove all ceiling sprinklers and rely on hose streams only
Ignore shelving because ESFR always suppresses without analysis

explanation: Solid shelving blocks ceiling water penetration; NFPA 13 storage sections require enhanced protection.

distractorExplanations.a: Greater ceiling height does not automatically reduce hazard.
distractorExplanations.c: Hose streams supplement but do not replace required automatic sprinklers.
distractorExplanations.d: ESFR applicability depends on commodity, height, and configuration analysis.
